title: Overheard acquisition talk
stem: During a plant tour hosted by a supplier, equity analyst Reyes overhears two of the supplier's executives discussing a takeover bid for Corvex Industries that has not been announced. Reyes verifies that no public source mentions the bid. Under the CFA Institute Standards of Professional Conduct, her most appropriate action is to:

choices.A: trade Corvex only in her personal account until the bid is announced
choices.B: refrain from acting on or passing along the information until it is made public
choices.C: combine the overheard remarks with public filings and issue a trade recommendation immediately

rationale: The overheard bid is material nonpublic information, so Standard II(A) bars acting on it or causing others to act until it is publicly disseminated. A is wrong because personal trades on material nonpublic information are exactly what Standard II(A) prohibits - the account used does not matter. C is wrong because the mosaic theory protects conclusions assembled from public and nonmaterial pieces, not recommendations that rest on a single material nonpublic fact.

title: Stray downgrade e-mail
stem: A credit analyst receives an e-mail from a company's investor-relations officer that was clearly sent to him by mistake: it contains a draft announcement that the company will be placed under review for a rating downgrade next week. Nothing about the review is public. The analyst's most appropriate response is to:

choices.A: wait until the review is publicly announced before using the information in any recommendation
choices.B: advise clients holding the bonds to reduce their positions, since his prior research already supported caution
choices.C: treat the draft as one nonmaterial input and blend it into his existing mosaic of public research

rationale: A stray internal draft of an unannounced ratings action is material nonpublic information, and Standard II(A) requires the analyst neither to act nor to cause others to act until the news is public. B is wrong because issuing the advice now would still put clients ahead of the market on the nonpublic review, whatever his prior research said. C is wrong because the draft is material on its own, so relabelling it as one mosaic input does not cure the violation.

title: Hot issue for the family
stem: A portfolio manager receives a small allocation of an oversubscribed initial public offering. Before distributing shares across the suitable client accounts on a pro rata basis, she first fills the accounts of two family members who are ordinary fee-paying clients of the firm. The Standard most directly violated is:

choices.A: Standard III(B) Fair Dealing
choices.B: Standard VI(B) Priority of Transactions
choices.C: Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity

rationale: Family accounts that are ordinary fee-paying clients must be treated exactly like every other client account, so filling them first denies the remaining clients fair access to the oversubscribed issue and violates the fair-dealing standard, III(B). B is wrong because Standard VI(B) governs sequencing between client trades and accounts in which the manager holds a beneficial interest, and these family accounts are regular client accounts. C is wrong because no outside pressure or inducement compromised her analysis - the failure is in allocation, not objectivity.

title: Tiered rating release
stem: An analyst upgrades a stock from hold to buy. His firm phones the change to its three largest institutional clients on Monday morning and e-mails the full client base on Tuesday afternoon. Retail clients who sold on Monday missed the price move. The Standard most directly violated is:

choices.A: Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care
choices.B: Standard III(B) Fair Dealing
choices.C: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis

rationale: Standard III(B) requires that recommendations be disseminated so all clients have a fair opportunity to act; giving three favoured clients a full day's head start is discriminatory dissemination. A is wrong because loyalty, prudence, and care concerns the duty owed within each client relationship, not the ordering of communications across clients. C is wrong because the upgrade itself may be perfectly well researched - the defect lies in how it was released, not in its basis.

title: Composite built from winners
stem: To market its balanced strategy, a firm claiming GIPS compliance builds the strategy composite from the five accounts with the best five-year records and leaves out eleven other accounts run to the same mandate. For the claim of compliance to be valid, the composite must instead contain:

choices.A: every actual, fee-paying, discretionary portfolio managed to the balanced mandate
choices.B: only the accounts the firm judges most representative of the strategy's potential
choices.C: all portfolios of the firm, including those whose clients direct the trades themselves

rationale: GIPS requires composites to include all actual, fee-paying, discretionary portfolios managed to the composite's strategy, precisely to prevent showcasing only the winners. B is wrong because letting the firm hand-pick representative accounts is the cherry-picking the requirement exists to stop. C is wrong because client-directed portfolios are non-discretionary and must be excluded - the firm does not control their investment decisions.

title: Which account joins the composite
stem: A GIPS-compliant firm defines a growth-equity composite. It manages three candidate accounts: a fee-paying pension account run with full discretion to the growth mandate; an account whose client dictates every trade; and an internal seed account that pays no fees. Which account MUST be included in the composite?

choices.A: the client-directed account, because it holds growth stocks
choices.B: the non-fee-paying seed account, because it is managed with discretion
choices.C: the discretionary, fee-paying pension account managed to the growth mandate

rationale: Composites must contain all actual, fee-paying, discretionary portfolios managed to the strategy, and only the pension account meets all three tests. A is wrong because an account whose client dictates every trade is non-discretionary and must be kept out of composites. B is wrong because non-fee-paying portfolios MAY be included with disclosure, but their inclusion is optional, never required.

title: Judging the manager, not the deposits
stem: A pension client wired a large contribution into its account two weeks before a strong market rally; the account's manager has no control over the timing of such flows. The consultant evaluating the MANAGER'S investment skill over the year should rely on:

choices.A: the money-weighted return, because it reflects every dollar the client invested
choices.B: the time-weighted return, because it strips out the effect of cash-flow timing the manager does not control
choices.C: the simple average of the twelve monthly holding-period returns

rationale: Time-weighted return chains sub-period returns and so removes the influence of externally timed contributions, which is exactly what isolating manager skill requires. A is wrong because the money-weighted return rewards or punishes the manager for the client's deposit timing - here it would flatter the manager for plain luck. C is wrong because a simple arithmetic average of monthly returns ignores compounding and is not the time-weighted measure.

title: Return on her own dollars
stem: Priya funds her brokerage account herself, choosing when to add savings and when to withdraw for expenses. She wants the single figure that best captures what HER invested dollars actually earned, including the consequences of her own timing decisions. She should compute:

choices.A: the time-weighted return
choices.B: the geometric mean of the account's calendar-quarter returns
choices.C: the money-weighted return, the internal rate of return of her account's cash flows

rationale: The money-weighted return is the internal rate of return of the account's actual contributions and withdrawals, so it credits or penalizes Priya's own timing - the question she is asking. A is wrong because the time-weighted return deliberately neutralizes cash-flow timing, discarding the very effect she wants to see. B is wrong because geometrically linking quarterly returns is a time-weighted calculation in another guise and equally ignores her flows.

title: A lucky rejection
stem: A researcher tests, at a 5% significance level, the null hypothesis that a trading strategy adds no alpha. Unknown to her, the null hypothesis is actually true, yet her sample leads the test to reject it. This outcome is best described as:

choices.A: a Type I error, whose probability was set in advance at the 5% significance level
choices.B: a Type II error, whose probability is denoted beta
choices.C: a Type I error, whose probability equals the power of the test

rationale: Rejecting a true null is a Type I error - a false positive - and the significance level alpha is precisely the probability the researcher chose in advance for that mistake. B is wrong because a Type II error is the opposite failure, not rejecting a false null. C is wrong because power is the probability of correctly rejecting a FALSE null (one minus beta), not the chance of this false alarm.

title: The miss that was not caught
stem: An auditor tests the null hypothesis that a lender's loan-default rate is at or below 2%. The true default rate is in fact well above 2%, but the test on her sample fails to reject the null. Her mistake and its probability are best labelled:

choices.A: Type I, with probability equal to the 5% significance level
choices.B: Type II, with probability denoted beta
choices.C: Type II, with probability equal to one minus beta

rationale: Failing to reject a false null is the definition of a Type II error, and its probability is beta; the test's power to catch the breach is one minus beta. A is wrong because a Type I error would require a TRUE null to be rejected - the reverse of what happened. C is wrong because one minus beta is the power, the probability of catching the false null rather than missing it.

title: Certificate with quarterly crediting
stem: Marta puts 25,000 into a savings certificate that pays a stated annual rate of 6% with interest credited every quarter, and she leaves the balance untouched for three years. The ending value is closest to:
rationale: The periodic rate is 6%/4 = 1.5% over 4 x 3 = 12 quarters, so the balance grows to 25,000 x 1.015^12 = 29,890. A is wrong because 29,500 is simple interest, 25,000 x (1 + 0.06 x 3), which earns nothing on prior interest. B is wrong because 29,775 compounds once a year at 6%, skipping the quarterly crediting the certificate specifies.

title: Pricing a five-payment stream
stem: A legal settlement entitles Daniel to receive 2,000 at the end of each of the next five years. An investor offers to buy the stream outright, pricing it at a 4% annual discount rate. The most the investor should pay today is closest to:
rationale: This is an ordinary annuity: 2,000 x (1 - 1.04^-5)/0.04 = 2,000 x 4.4518 = 8,904. B is wrong because 9,260 treats the payments as arriving at the START of each year (an annuity due), granting one extra year of interest relief the settlement does not give. C is wrong because 10,000 simply adds the five payments with no discounting at all.

title: Rice when incomes rise
stem: Household incomes in a region rise 8% and, with prices unchanged, the quantity of store-brand rice demanded falls 4%. For these consumers, store-brand rice is best classified as:

choices.A: a normal good with income elasticity of +0.5
choices.B: an inferior good with income elasticity of -0.5
choices.C: a complement good with cross-price elasticity of -0.5

rationale: Income elasticity is the percentage change in quantity demanded over the percentage change in income: -4%/8% = -0.5, and a negative value defines an inferior good - richer households buy less of it. A is wrong on the sign: quantity fell while income rose. C is wrong because cross-price elasticity relates quantity to another good's PRICE, and no other price changed here.

title: Dining out on a raise
stem: After average incomes in a city climb 5%, restaurant visits per household rise 12% with menu prices flat. Restaurant meals in this city are best described as:

choices.A: a normal good, with an income elasticity of about 2.4
choices.B: an inferior good, because spending rose faster than income
choices.C: a substitute good, with a cross-price elasticity of about 2.4

rationale: Income elasticity = 12%/5% = 2.4; a positive value marks a normal good, and a value above one makes demand income-elastic, luxury-like. B is wrong because inferior goods see quantity FALL as income rises - the opposite happened, and outpacing income growth does not make a good inferior. C is wrong because substitutes are identified from the response to another good's price, which never moved.

title: Crowded field of roasters
stem: In a city, dozens of small coffee roasters sell distinct house blends, each cultivating its own brand and enjoying modest control over its prices; new roasters open frequently with little difficulty. This market is best characterized as:

choices.A: perfect competition
choices.B: monopolistic competition
choices.C: oligopoly

rationale: Many sellers, differentiated products, low entry barriers, and limited individual pricing power are the defining features of monopolistic competition. A is wrong because perfect competition requires homogeneous products and price-taking firms, while these roasters differentiate and set their own prices. C is wrong because an oligopoly has few, strategically interdependent firms behind high entry barriers, not dozens of easy entrants.

title: Three carriers, one spectrum auction
stem: A country's mobile-network market is served by three carriers. Spectrum licences and network build-outs make entry extremely costly, and each carrier sets prices only after weighing how the other two will respond. This market structure is best classified as:

choices.A: monopolistic competition
choices.B: monopoly
choices.C: oligopoly

rationale: A handful of firms, prohibitive entry barriers, and pricing driven by rivals' expected reactions - strategic interdependence - define an oligopoly. A is wrong because monopolistic competition needs many sellers and easy entry, neither present here. B is wrong because a monopoly is a single seller, and this market has three.

title: Costing the warehouse sale
stem: A distributor began the period holding 100 units that had cost $10 each, then bought 100 more units at $12 each as prices rose. It sold 150 units and applies LIFO with a periodic system. The period's cost of goods sold under this method comes to approximately:
rationale: LIFO charges the newest costs to cost of goods sold: all 100 units at $12 (1,200) plus 50 units at $10 (500) gives $1,700. A is wrong because $1,600 costs the sale oldest-first (100 x $10 + 50 x $12), which is the FIFO result. B is wrong because $1,650 applies the $11 weighted-average unit cost (2,200/200) to the 150 units instead of LIFO layers.

title: What remains on the shelf
stem: A retailer entered the season with 60 units that had cost €5 apiece and later purchased 90 more at €8 apiece. During the season it sold 100 units. Under FIFO with a periodic system, the ending inventory balance is closest to:
rationale: FIFO sells the oldest layers first, so the 50 unsold units all come from the €8 purchase layer: 50 x €8 = €400. A is wrong because €250 prices the remainder from the oldest €5 layer, which is LIFO's ending inventory. B is wrong because €340 applies the €6.80 weighted-average unit cost (1,020/150) instead of FIFO layering.

title: Screening without the stockroom
stem: A credit analyst reviews a firm reporting cash of 40, short-term marketable securities of 20, receivables of 60, inventory of 80, prepaid expenses of 10, and current liabilities of 100. The firm's quick ratio is closest to:
rationale: The quick ratio keeps only the most liquid current assets: (40 + 20 + 60)/100 = 1.2, leaving out inventory and prepaids. A is wrong because 0.6 is the cash ratio, (40 + 20)/100, which also drops receivables. C is wrong because 2.1 is the current ratio, (40 + 20 + 60 + 80 + 10)/100, which counts the inventory the quick ratio exists to exclude.

title: Liquidity after doubting the stock
stem: Worried that a wholesaler's merchandise is slow-moving, an analyst wants the liquidity measure that ignores inventory entirely but still counts receivables. The wholesaler shows cash 15, short-term investments 5, trade receivables 30, inventory 70, and current liabilities 50. That measure equals approximately:
rationale: Excluding inventory while keeping receivables is the quick ratio: (15 + 5 + 30)/50 = 1.0. B is wrong because 0.4 is the cash ratio, (15 + 5)/50, which throws out the receivables as well - stricter than what was asked. C is wrong because 2.4 is the current ratio, (15 + 5 + 30 + 70)/50, which includes the very inventory the analyst distrusts.

title: Averaging the bin
stem: A parts supplier began with 200 units carried at $3.00 each and purchased 300 more during the period at $4.00 each. It sold 350 units. Under the weighted-average cost method, ending inventory is closest to:
rationale: Average unit cost = (200 x 3.00 + 300 x 4.00)/500 = $3.60, and 150 units remain: 150 x 3.60 = $540. A is wrong because $450 prices the remainder at the oldest $3.00 cost, the LIFO ending inventory. C is wrong because $600 prices it at the newest $4.00 cost, the FIFO ending inventory.

title: Blended cost of the quarter's shipments
stem: A chemicals firm started the quarter with 80 drums at $12.50 each and added 120 drums at $15.00 each. It shipped 130 drums to customers. Using the weighted-average cost method, its cost of goods sold is closest to:
rationale: Average cost per drum = (80 x 12.50 + 120 x 15.00)/200 = $14.00, so cost of goods sold = 130 x 14.00 = $1,820. A is wrong because $1,750 charges the oldest layers first (80 x 12.50 + 50 x 15.00), the FIFO figure. C is wrong because $1,925 charges the newest first (120 x 15.00 + 10 x 12.50), the LIFO figure.

title: Two projects, one site
stem: A firm with a 10% cost of capital must pick exactly one of two mutually exclusive projects for a single site. Project X has an NPV of 4.2 million and an IRR of 18%; Project Y has an NPV of 5.6 million and an IRR of 14%. The firm should choose:

choices.A: Project X, because its IRR is higher
choices.B: either project, because both IRRs exceed the 10% cost of capital
choices.C: Project Y, because its NPV is higher

rationale: For mutually exclusive projects the NPV rule governs: NPV measures value added at the firm's actual cost of capital and assumes reinvestment at that rate, so Y's 5.6 million beats X's 4.2 million. A is wrong because IRR can favour smaller or front-loaded projects and assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself - the classic ranking conflict. B is wrong because clearing the hurdle only makes each acceptable in isolation; exclusivity forces the value-maximizing choice.

title: Bakery oven or warehouse
stem: A bakery chain can fund only one expansion this year. Refitting its ovens costs little and shows an IRR of 30% with an NPV of 1.1 million; building a regional warehouse shows an IRR of 16% with an NPV of 3.4 million. Both use the same 9% discount rate. To maximize shareholder value the chain should:

choices.A: refit the ovens, since a 30% IRR signals the most efficient use of capital
choices.B: build the warehouse, since it adds more present value
choices.C: reject both, since such a wide IRR spread signals inconsistent risk

rationale: When project scale differs, the higher-IRR project can still add less wealth; NPV in currency terms is what shareholders keep, and 3.4 million exceeds 1.1 million. A is wrong because IRR is a rate, not an amount - a high return on a small base creates less value here. C is wrong because both projects are positive-NPV at the appropriate 9% rate; differing IRRs across projects of different size are normal, not a risk signal.

title: Blending the funding mix
stem: A company targets a capital structure of 60% equity and 40% debt. Its cost of equity is 12%, its pre-tax cost of debt is 7%, and its marginal tax rate is 25%. Its weighted average cost of capital is closest to:
rationale: WACC = 0.60 x 12% + 0.40 x 7% x (1 - 0.25) = 7.2% + 2.1% = 9.3%; only the debt cost is tax-adjusted because interest is deductible. A is wrong because 7.5% applies the tax shield to the equity term as well - dividends are not deductible. C is wrong because 10.0% forgets the tax shield entirely and uses the pre-tax 7% cost of debt.

title: Cost of funds at market weights
stem: An analyst values a firm whose equity has a market value of 250 million and whose debt also trades at a market value of 250 million. The required return on equity is 10%, the yield on the debt is 6%, and the tax rate is 30%. The firm's after-tax weighted average cost of capital is closest to:
rationale: Market-value weights are 50/50: 0.5 x 10% + 0.5 x 6% x (1 - 0.30) = 5.0% + 2.1% = 7.1%. A is wrong because 5.6% tax-adjusts the equity return too (0.5 x 10% x 0.7 + 2.1%), and equity costs carry no tax shield. C is wrong because 8.0% ignores the interest tax shield and uses the full 6% debt yield.

title: Profits the hypothesis permits
stem: Assume a stock market is efficient in the semi-strong form. Which source of consistently abnormal returns remains possible under that assumption?

choices.A: trading on material information about an unannounced merger known only to insiders
choices.B: fundamental screens built from published financial statements
choices.C: chart-based rules built from past prices and trading volume

rationale: Semi-strong efficiency says prices already impound all PUBLIC information; it is silent about private information, so insider knowledge could still earn abnormal returns - which is why laws, not market forces, police it. B is wrong because published statements are public information, already priced under the assumption. C is wrong because past prices and volume are also public: the weak-form information set is contained in the semi-strong set.

title: What the evidence pattern implies
stem: In a market, returns from analyzing published filings are no better than chance, yet corporate insiders systematically profit trading ahead of their own announcements. Taken together, these facts are most consistent with prices reflecting:

choices.A: all public information but not all private information
choices.B: all information, public and private
choices.C: less than the full set of past price information

rationale: Public-data analysis failing means the semi-strong form holds; insiders profiting means private information is NOT fully in prices, so the strong form fails - exactly option A. B is wrong because insider profits directly contradict the strong form. C is wrong because nothing shown suggests past-price rules work: if the semi-strong form holds, the weak form holds too.

title: Steady grower, next dividend known
stem: A utility is expected to pay a dividend of 2.40 per share ONE YEAR from now, and dividends are expected to grow at 5% per year indefinitely. Shareholders require an 11% return. The stock's value per share today is closest to:
rationale: Gordon growth: P0 = D1/(r - g) = 2.40/(0.11 - 0.05) = 2.40/0.06 = 40.00. A is wrong because 15.00 divides by r + g = 16% instead of the r - g spread. B is wrong because 21.82 divides by the full 11% required return, ignoring growth altogether.

title: Valuing on a dividend just paid
stem: A consumer-staples firm has JUST PAID an annual dividend of 3.00 per share. Analysts expect dividends to grow 4% a year forever, and the required return on the shares is 10%. Using the constant-growth model, the share value today is closest to:
rationale: The model needs next year's dividend: D1 = 3.00 x 1.04 = 3.12, so P0 = 3.12/(0.10 - 0.04) = 52.00. A is wrong because 31.20 divides D1 by the full 10% required return instead of the 6% spread. B is wrong because 50.00 puts the just-paid 3.00 in the numerator - the D0-for-D1 trap.

title: Whose move drags the average
stem: An index of two stocks is computed by summing their share prices and dividing by a fixed divisor. Stock G trades at 80 but is a small company; stock H trades at 20 but has forty times G's market value. A 5% one-day move in which stock changes the index more?

choices.A: stock H, because its market capitalization is far larger
choices.B: each moves the index equally, because the index holds one share of each
choices.C: stock G, because the index weights by price and 5% of 80 is the larger price change

rationale: In a price-weighted average, impact is proportional to share price: 5% of 80 shifts the numerator by 4.0 versus 1.0 for stock H, regardless of company size. A is wrong because market capitalization drives cap-weighted indexes, not price-weighted ones. B is wrong because holding one share of each is exactly why the higher-priced share dominates percentage moves - equal impact would require equal prices.

title: Index arithmetic after one riser
stem: A price-weighted index contains three stocks closing yesterday at 25, 40, and 85, with a divisor of 3. Today the 25 stock rises 8% to 27 while the other two are unchanged. The index's one-day return is closest to:
rationale: The index moves from 150/3 = 50.00 to 152/3 = 50.67, a gain of 2/150 = 1.33%. B is wrong because +2.67% averages the three stocks' percentage returns (8%, 0%, 0%) equally, an equal-weighted calculation rather than a price-weighted one. C is wrong because +8.00% is the single stock's own return, ignoring the two flat index members.

title: From time measure to rate sensitivity
stem: A corporate bond pays coupons twice a year. Its Macaulay duration is 8.40 years and its yield to maturity is 6.0%, quoted as an annual rate with semiannual compounding. The bond's modified duration, in years, is closest to:
rationale: With semiannual compounding the divisor uses the periodic yield: 8.40/(1 + 0.06/2) = 8.40/1.03 = 8.16. A is wrong because 7.92 divides by 1.06, applying a full year's yield where the six-month rate belongs. C is wrong because 8.40 is the unconverted Macaulay figure - a time measure, not the rate sensitivity requested.

title: Annual payer, annual divisor
stem: A sovereign bond pays one coupon per year. Its Macaulay duration is 11.25 years and its yield to maturity is 8.0% with annual compounding. Its modified duration, in years, is closest to:
rationale: For an annual-pay bond the periodic yield IS the annual yield: 11.25/1.08 = 10.42. B is wrong because 10.82 divides by 1.04 as though coupons were semiannual - the divisor's compounding must match the bond, and this one pays annually. C is wrong because 11.25 leaves the Macaulay duration unconverted.

title: Sizing the hit from higher yields
stem: A bond has a Macaulay duration of 6.70 and a modified duration of 6.50. Its yield to maturity rises by 50 basis points. Using the first-order duration estimate, the bond's percentage price change is closest to:
rationale: The linear estimate multiplies MODIFIED duration by the yield change: -6.50 x 0.0050 = -3.25%. B is wrong because -3.35% plugs in the Macaulay 6.70 and overstates the move - Macaulay is a time measure, not the sensitivity coefficient. C is wrong on direction: prices fall when yields rise, so the sign must be negative.

title: Currency impact of a rate dip
stem: A portfolio holds 2,000,000 of an option-free bond position with a modified duration of 4.8. Market yields FALL by 25 basis points. The approximate change in the position's value is closest to:

choices.A: a gain of 24,000
choices.B: a loss of 24,000
choices.C: a gain of 48,000

rationale: Value change is roughly position x modified duration x yield change = 2,000,000 x 4.8 x 0.0025 = 24,000, and falling yields raise prices, so it is a gain. B is wrong because it flips the price-yield relationship - a yield drop cannot produce a duration loss on a long position. C is wrong because 48,000 uses 50 basis points, double the stated move.

title: Stripping out the call
stem: A callable corporate bond shows a Z-spread of 180 basis points over the government spot curve. The embedded call option's cost is estimated at 45 basis points. The bond's option-adjusted spread is closest to:

choices.A: 135 basis points
choices.B: 180 basis points
choices.C: 225 basis points

rationale: For a callable bond the OAS removes the compensation for the option the investor has effectively sold: OAS = Z-spread minus option cost = 180 - 45 = 135. B is wrong because 180 is the unadjusted Z-spread, which still bundles the option compensation with credit compensation. C is wrong because adding the option cost goes the wrong way - the call hurts the bondholder, so the pure credit spread must be SMALLER than the Z-spread.

title: Rebuilding the all-in spread
stem: An analyst models a callable bank bond and estimates its option-adjusted spread at 110 basis points, with the embedded call worth 60 basis points. Consistent with these figures, the bond's Z-spread over the spot curve is closest to:

choices.A: 50 basis points
choices.B: 110 basis points
choices.C: 170 basis points

rationale: The Z-spread of a callable bond equals its OAS plus the option cost: 110 + 60 = 170 basis points, since holders demand extra spread for the call they are short. A is wrong because subtracting the option cost from the OAS removes the option twice. B is wrong because 110 is the OAS itself; the all-in Z-spread must sit ABOVE it for a callable bond.

title: One tailored delivery
stem: An airline wants to lock in the price of one specific jet-fuel delivery: an unusual quantity, at a minor airport, on a date that matches no listed contract. It accepts counterparty credit exposure and prefers no cash flows before delivery. The instrument that best fits is:

choices.A: an exchange-traded futures contract
choices.B: an over-the-counter forward contract
choices.C: a commodity swap

rationale: A forward is a private, fully customizable commitment settling once at delivery - matching the odd size, location, and date and the no-interim-cash-flow preference, at the price of bearing counterparty risk. A is wrong because listed futures are standardized in size, grade, and date and are marked to market daily with margin calls. C is wrong because a swap exchanges payments over a SCHEDULE of dates, suiting recurring exposure rather than a single delivery.

title: Why the clearinghouse comforts her
stem: A grain trader is deciding between a forward from a regional dealer and an equivalent exchange-listed futures contract. Her overriding concern is the risk that her counterparty fails to pay after a large adverse move. Futures address this concern chiefly because:

choices.A: futures eliminate the price risk of the hedge itself
choices.B: a central clearinghouse guarantees performance and daily settlement keeps losses from accumulating
choices.C: futures can be customized so the dealer bears all default risk

rationale: The clearinghouse interposes itself as counterparty to every trade, backed by margin, and daily marking to market realizes gains and losses before they can grow - the core credit-risk machinery of futures. A is wrong because both instruments carry the same market-price exposure; futures change WHO bears default risk, not price risk. C is wrong because customization is the forward's feature, and it deepens reliance on the dealer's credit rather than removing it.

title: Netting out the option's cost
stem: Three months ago Lena paid a premium of 3.00 for a call option with a strike of 50 on a stock that trades at 58 at expiration. Ignoring transaction costs, her PROFIT per share at expiration is closest to:
rationale: The exercise value is max(0, 58 - 50) = 8.00, and profit nets out the premium: 8.00 - 3.00 = 5.00. B is wrong because 8.00 is the payoff alone - the question asks for profit, which must deduct what she paid. C is wrong because 11.00 ADDS the premium to the payoff instead of subtracting it.

title: What the put delivers at the bell
stem: Marco bought a put option with a strike of 40 for a premium of 2.50. At expiration the underlying stock trades at 33. The option's PAYOFF - its exercise value, not his profit - per share at expiration is closest to:
rationale: A put pays max(0, X - S) = max(0, 40 - 33) = 7.00 at expiration. A is wrong because 4.50 subtracts the 2.50 premium, converting the payoff into PROFIT, which is not what was asked. C is wrong because 9.50 adds the premium to the exercise value, a sum with no financial meaning.

title: Curve above the barrel
stem: Spot crude oil trades at 70.00 while the three-month futures contract trades at 74.00. An index fund holds a long position that it must roll forward every quarter. This market shape and its consequence for the fund are best described as:

choices.A: backwardation, producing a positive roll yield for the long position
choices.B: contango, producing a negative roll yield for the long position
choices.C: contango, producing a positive roll yield for the long position

rationale: Futures above spot defines contango, and rolling a long position in contango means repeatedly buying dearer deferred contracts whose prices converge down toward spot - a negative roll yield. A is wrong because backwardation means futures BELOW spot, the opposite of these prices. C is wrong because it pairs the right curve shape with the wrong consequence: contango penalizes a rolling long, it does not reward it.

title: Wheat curve sloping down
stem: Cash wheat changes hands at 6.20 per bushel while the deferred futures contract trades at 5.80. A commodity fund maintains a continuously rolled LONG futures exposure to wheat. The market state and the roll's effect on the fund are best described as:

choices.A: backwardation, adding a positive roll yield
choices.B: contango, imposing a negative roll yield
choices.C: backwardation, imposing a negative roll yield

rationale: Futures priced below the cash market is backwardation; a long roller sells the expiring contract near the higher spot and buys the cheaper deferred one, harvesting a positive roll yield as prices converge upward. B is wrong because contango would require futures ABOVE the 6.20 cash price, not at 5.80. C is wrong because it attaches the penalty to the wrong curve shape - the negative roll belongs to contango.

title: Carving up a good year
stem: A hedge fund starts the year at its high-water mark with 100 million in assets and earns gross returns that lift assets to 120 million before any fees. Terms are a 2% management fee on END-of-year gross assets and a 20% incentive fee on gains above the high-water mark measured NET of the management fee. Total fees for the year are closest to:

choices.A: 4.00 million
choices.B: 5.92 million
choices.C: 6.40 million

rationale: Management fee = 2% x 120 = 2.40 million; the incentive base is 120 - 2.40 - 100 = 17.60 million, so the incentive fee is 20% x 17.60 = 3.52 million, for 5.92 million in total. A is wrong because 4.00 million is an incentive fee taken on the raw 20 million gain with no management fee at all. C is wrong because 6.40 million charges 20% on the gross gain (4.00) plus the 2.40 management fee, ignoring that the incentive base is net of the management fee.

title: A rebound that pays no bonus
stem: A fund charges a 1.5% management fee on end-of-year assets plus a 20% incentive fee, subject to a high-water mark of 250 million set two years ago. After a losing stretch, assets climb from 210 million to 240 million by year-end. Total fees for THIS year are closest to:

choices.A: 3.60 million
choices.B: 6.00 million
choices.C: 9.60 million

rationale: Assets finish below the 250 million high-water mark, so no incentive fee is due; only the management fee applies: 1.5% x 240 = 3.60 million. B is wrong because 6.00 million takes 20% of the 30 million rebound, ignoring that the high-water mark blocks incentive fees until 250 is regained. C is wrong because 9.60 million charges both the management fee and that blocked incentive fee.

title: Forecast against the line
stem: The risk-free rate is 3% and the expected return on the market portfolio is 9%. An analyst's model projects an 11% return for a stock with a beta of 1.2. Relative to the security market line, the stock is best judged:

choices.A: undervalued, because the projected 11% exceeds the 10.2% CAPM-required return
choices.B: overvalued, because the CAPM-required return is 13.8%
choices.C: overvalued, because its total volatility exceeds the market's

rationale: Required return = 3% + 1.2 x (9% - 3%) = 10.2%; an 11% forecast plots ABOVE the security market line - positive alpha, hence undervalued. B is wrong because 13.8% treats the 9% market RETURN as if it were the market risk premium; the premium is 9% - 3% = 6%. C is wrong because the SML prices only systematic (beta) risk - total volatility belongs to the capital market line, and nothing here measures it.

title: Priced off the premium
stem: The risk-free rate is 2% and the market risk premium is 7%. A stock with a beta of 0.8 is forecast by an analyst to return 6.5%. According to the CAPM and the security market line, the stock is:

choices.A: undervalued, because the required return is only 6.0%
choices.B: overvalued, because the forecast falls short of the 7.6% required return
choices.C: impossible to judge without the stock's standard deviation

rationale: The 7% is already the PREMIUM, so the required return = 2% + 0.8 x 7% = 7.6%; a 6.5% forecast plots below the security market line - negative alpha, overvalued. A is wrong because 6.0% subtracts the risk-free rate from the premium again (2% + 0.8 x 5%), double-counting it. C is wrong because CAPM pricing needs beta only; standard deviation matters for total-risk measures, not the SML.

title: Reward per unit of total risk
stem: Over the year a balanced fund returned 12% with a standard deviation of 16% and a beta of 0.9, while the risk-free rate was 4%. The fund's Sharpe ratio for the year is closest to:
rationale: Sharpe = (12% - 4%)/16% = 0.50, excess return per unit of TOTAL risk. B is wrong because 0.75 divides the raw 12% return by volatility without first subtracting the risk-free rate. C is wrong because 8.89 divides the excess return by beta - that is the Treynor measure, scaled to systematic risk, not the Sharpe ratio.

title: Reward per unit of beta
stem: A sector fund earned 10% over a year in which the risk-free rate was 2%. Its return standard deviation was 20% and its beta was 1.25. The fund's TREYNOR measure for the year is closest to:
rationale: Treynor = (10% - 2%)/1.25 = 6.40 percentage points of excess return per unit of beta. A is wrong because 0.40 divides the excess return by the 20% standard deviation, which is the Sharpe ratio. C is wrong because 8.00 divides the RAW return by beta, forgetting to remove the risk-free rate first.

title: Borrowed model, missing credit
stem: Preparing a client research note, an analyst finds a clear description of a valuation framework in another firm's published white paper. He rewrites a few sentences lightly, presents the framework as his own design, and cites nothing. The Standard most directly violated is:

choices.A: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis
choices.B: Standard VII(B) Reference to CFA Institute, the CFA Designation, and the Program
choices.C: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation

rationale: Passing off another firm's framework as his own design - even lightly reworded - is plagiarism, a misrepresentation under Standard I(C). A is wrong because the depth of his research effort is not the issue; the false claim of authorship is. B is wrong because Standard VII(B) governs how members describe the CFA designation and program, which never arises here.

title: Outlook lifted from a rival
stem: A strategist joins a new employer and republishes, nearly word for word, the capital-market outlook her former team wrote last year - under her sole byline, with no attribution to the source document or its authors. The Standard most directly violated is:

choices.A: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation
choices.B: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation
choices.C: Standard IV(A) Loyalty (duties to employers)

rationale: Republishing others' written work under her own name without credit is plagiarism, which Standard I(C) treats as misrepresentation regardless of where she now works. B is wrong because Standard III(D) concerns the fair presentation of investment RESULTS, and no performance record is being shown. C is wrong because the live violation in the published note is the false claim of authorship, not a duty owed to the employer she left.

title: Three uneven years, one rate
stem: A portfolio returns +20% in year one, -10% in year two, and +15% in year three, with no external cash flows. The compound annual growth rate over the three years is closest to:
rationale: The geometric mean links the growth factors: (1.20 x 0.90 x 1.15)^(1/3) - 1 = 1.242^(1/3) - 1 = 7.49%. B is wrong because 8.33% is the arithmetic mean of the three returns, which ignores compounding and overstates growth whenever returns vary. C is wrong because 8.07% divides the 24.2% total holding-period return by three - neither mean, and still no compounding.

title: Up ten, down ten
stem: An account worth 100,000 gains 10% in the first period and then loses 10% in the second, with no deposits or withdrawals. Its value at the end of the second period is closest to:
rationale: Value compounds multiplicatively: 100,000 x 1.10 x 0.90 = 99,000 - the volatility drag that pulls the geometric mean below the arithmetic mean (which is 0% here). B is wrong because it trusts the 0% arithmetic average; equal up and down percentages do not cancel. C is wrong because no ordering of a +10% and a -10% period can leave the account above its starting value.

title: Peg with the gates open
stem: A small economy pegs its currency to the US dollar and allows capital to flow freely across its border. Domestic unemployment rises and the government wants aggressive interest-rate cuts. Within this regime, the central bank:

choices.A: can cut rates freely, because a peg constrains only fiscal policy
choices.B: cannot run an independent interest-rate policy, because rates must defend the peg
choices.C: can cut rates as long as the budget deficit is capped at the same time

rationale: With a fixed exchange rate and open capital markets, domestic rates must shadow the anchor currency's rates or capital flight breaks the peg - monetary independence is the corner of the policy trilemma this country surrendered. A is wrong because the peg binds MONETARY policy specifically, not fiscal policy. C is wrong because capping the deficit is a fiscal action and does nothing to stop the outflows that rate cuts would trigger.

title: Holding the line on the peg
stem: Speculators begin selling a country's pegged currency, pushing it toward the weak edge of its band against the anchor currency. To maintain the peg in the near term, the central bank most likely must:

choices.A: buy its own currency with foreign-exchange reserves and accept higher domestic interest rates
choices.B: sell its own currency and cut its policy rate
choices.C: announce fiscal stimulus to attract foreign investment

rationale: Defending a peg against depreciation means absorbing the selling pressure: buying the domestic currency with reserves and letting rates rise so holding the currency stays attractive. B is wrong because selling one's own currency and cutting rates ADDS to the depreciation pressure. C is wrong because fiscal announcements work slowly and indirectly; a peg is defended with reserves and interest rates, the tools the central bank actually controls.

title: Margins when costs climb
stem: A wholesaler's purchase costs rose steadily all year while its selling prices and unit volumes held constant, and inventory quantities were stable. Holding everything else equal, the inventory cost method that reports the HIGHEST gross profit for the year is:

choices.A: LIFO
choices.B: the weighted-average cost method
choices.C: FIFO

rationale: With costs rising, FIFO charges the oldest, cheapest units to cost of goods sold, so its cost of sales is the lowest and its gross profit the highest. A is wrong because LIFO expenses the newest, most expensive units, producing the LOWEST gross profit in this environment. B is wrong because the weighted-average result lands between the two extremes, so it cannot be the highest.

title: Deflation flips the ranking
stem: A components distributor operates through a year in which its suppliers cut prices every quarter; sales volumes and inventory quantities stay steady. All else equal, the cost-flow method that reports the LOWEST cost of goods sold for that year is:

choices.A: FIFO
choices.B: LIFO
choices.C: the weighted-average cost method

rationale: When purchase costs are FALLING, the newest units are the cheapest, and LIFO is the method that sends the newest costs to cost of goods sold - so LIFO now reports the lowest figure. A is wrong because FIFO charges the older, more expensive units in a deflationary run. C is wrong because the weighted average again lands between the extremes; the familiar rising-price ranking simply reverses.

title: How levered are the operations
stem: When a machine-tool maker's unit sales rise 10%, its operating income rises 25%, an amplification driven by its fixed-cost base. The firm's degree of operating leverage at this level of output is closest to:
rationale: Degree of operating leverage = percentage change in operating income over percentage change in units sold = 25%/10% = 2.5. A is wrong because 0.4 inverts the ratio (10/25). C is wrong because 15.0 subtracts the two percentages instead of dividing them - leverage is a ratio of sensitivities, not a spread.

title: Downturn through the cost structure
stem: A packaging firm's degree of operating leverage is 3.0. Management projects a 4% decline in unit sales next year with selling prices unchanged. The projected change in operating income is closest to:
rationale: Operating income moves by DOL times the sales change: 3.0 x (-4%) = -12%. A is wrong because -4% ignores the leverage entirely, as if every cost were variable. B is wrong because -7% adds the leverage factor to the sales change instead of multiplying by it.

title: A rule the weak form forbids
stem: Assume a stock market is efficient in the WEAK form only. Which approach should be UNABLE to deliver consistently abnormal returns?

choices.A: buy and sell signals generated from moving averages of past prices
choices.B: screens on newly released, not-yet-digested financial disclosures
choices.C: positions taken on private knowledge of an unannounced tender offer

rationale: Weak-form efficiency says past prices and volume are already reflected in current prices, so mechanical rules built from them - like moving-average signals - cannot persistently win. B is wrong because weak-form-ONLY efficiency leaves room for public fundamental information to be exploited before prices adjust. C is wrong because private information sits outside even the semi-strong set; the weak form says nothing that rules it out.

title: Momentum that will not die
stem: A study documents that portfolios of stocks with the highest returns over the past year keep beating the market for months afterward, net of trading costs and risk adjustment, decade after decade. This evidence most directly contradicts which form of market efficiency?

choices.A: the weak form
choices.B: the strong form only
choices.C: none - persistent momentum is consistent with weak-form efficiency

rationale: Momentum strategies use only past RETURNS, exactly the information set the weak form claims is already in prices, so durable net-of-cost momentum profits contradict the weak form directly. B is wrong because the weak-form claim is the specific one broken; its failure then propagates upward to the stronger forms, not the other way around. C is wrong because persistently profiting from past prices is precisely what weak-form efficiency rules out.

title: Sensitivity for a bond that may be called
stem: A portfolio manager needs the interest-rate sensitivity of a corporate bond that the issuer can call at par in two years. Because a rate drop could trigger the call and change the bond's cash flows, the appropriate duration measure is:

choices.A: modified duration
choices.B: Macaulay duration
choices.C: effective duration

rationale: Effective duration reprices the bond under up and down shifts of the benchmark curve, letting the modelled cash flows respond to rates - required when a call may shorten the bond. A is wrong because modified duration is a yield-based statistic that presumes FIXED cash flows, which the call clause breaks. B is wrong because Macaulay duration is a weighted time-to-cash-flow measure, not a price-sensitivity statistic for option-bearing bonds.

title: When two durations tell one story
stem: A junior analyst insists that effective duration must be computed for every bond a desk holds. For which holding would modified duration and effective duration give nearly the same answer, making the extra curve-shift computation unnecessary?

choices.A: a callable corporate bond trading near its call price
choices.B: an agency mortgage-backed security
choices.C: an option-free fixed-coupon government bond

rationale: With no embedded options the cash flows do not change as rates move, so the yield-based (modified) and curve-shift (effective) measures nearly coincide and the extra computation adds little. A is wrong because near the call price the option dominates and the two measures diverge sharply. B is wrong because mortgage prepayments shift cash flows with rates - exactly the case that demands effective duration.

title: Backing out the put
stem: European options on a non-dividend stock trade with one year to expiration. The stock is at 95.00, the strike is 100.00, the annual risk-free rate is 4%, and the call sells for 6.20. By put-call parity, the put's price is closest to:
rationale: Parity gives p = c + X/(1 + r)^T - S = 6.20 + 100/1.04 - 95 = 6.20 + 96.15 - 95 = 7.35. A is wrong because 5.05 flips the signs on the stock and the discounted strike (6.20 - 96.15 + 95). C is wrong because 11.20 uses the undiscounted 100 strike, skipping the present-value step parity requires.

title: Backing out the call
stem: A European put on a non-dividend stock sells for 1.35 with one year to run. The stock trades at 60.00, the strike is 55.00, and the one-year risk-free rate is 6%. Using put-call parity, the matching call's price is closest to:
rationale: Parity rearranges to c = p + S - X/(1 + r)^T = 1.35 + 60 - 55/1.06 = 1.35 + 60 - 51.89 = 9.46. A is wrong because 6.35 fails to discount the 55 strike back one year. B is wrong because 8.11 is S minus the discounted strike alone, dropping the put premium from the identity.

title: Cheques for the unproven
stem: A private fund buys minority equity stakes in young software companies that have prototypes but little or no revenue, uses no debt, and expects most positions to fail while a few return many multiples. This strategy is best described as:

choices.A: a leveraged buyout strategy
choices.B: venture capital
choices.C: distressed-debt investing

rationale: Minority stakes, early-stage unproven companies, no leverage, and a hit-driven return profile are the signature of venture capital. A is wrong because buyouts take CONTROL of mature, cash-generating businesses and finance the purchase heavily with debt. C is wrong because distressed investing buys the obligations of troubled established firms, not equity in young ones.

title: Whole company, mostly borrowed
stem: A private-equity fund acquires 100% of a mature industrial laundry chain with steady cash flows, funding 65% of the purchase price with debt secured by the target's own assets, and plans operational improvements before an exit in five years. This transaction is best classified as:

choices.A: a leveraged buyout
choices.B: a venture capital investment
choices.C: an investment in a fund of funds

rationale: Control of a mature, cash-generative business bought predominantly with debt serviced by the target's own cash flows is the definition of a leveraged buyout. B is wrong because venture capital takes minority positions in early-stage companies and rarely uses leverage. C is wrong because a fund of funds buys stakes in OTHER FUNDS, not operating companies.

title: What forty tickets diversify away
stem: An investor's entire portfolio is one biotech stock. She replaces it with a portfolio of forty stocks spread across unrelated industries, keeping the same total amount invested. The risk this change primarily reduces is:

choices.A: market-wide (systematic) risk
choices.B: firm-specific (unsystematic) risk
choices.C: both kinds of risk, in equal proportion

rationale: Diversification averages away shocks unique to individual companies - failed trials, lawsuits, management stumbles - which is the unsystematic component. A is wrong because market-wide movements hit all forty stocks together and survive any amount of diversification. C is wrong because only the firm-specific part shrinks while the systematic part is untouched, so the reduction is anything but equal.

title: Which risk earns the premium
stem: Stock U, a utility, has high total return volatility but a beta of 0.6; stock T, a technology firm, has lower total volatility but a beta of 1.4. Under the CAPM, which stock must offer the higher expected return in equilibrium, and why?

choices.A: stock U, because total volatility is what investors are compensated for
choices.B: stock T, because expected return is priced on systematic (beta) risk
choices.C: both the same, because their risks offset across a diversified portfolio

rationale: The CAPM compensates only non-diversifiable risk: expected return rises with beta, so the 1.4-beta technology stock must offer more. A is wrong because the diversifiable part of the utility's volatility earns nothing - holders can shed it for free by diversifying. C is wrong because equal expected returns at different betas would create an arbitrage against the security market line, not an equilibrium.
